sentences.base_language: One morning, Junhyeok woke up with a headache and a stomachache, and felt very tired.
sentences.target_language: 어느 날 아침, 준혁은 머리와 배가 아파서 너무 피곤함을 느꼈다.
sentences.romanized: Eoneu nal achim, Junhyeok-eun meori wa bae ga apaseo neomu pigonham-eul neukkkyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**머리/배가 아프다** = head/stomach hurts: in Korean the body part is the subject (marked **이/가**) of 아프다 (to hurt/be sick): 머리**가** 아프다 (head hurts), 배**가** 아프다 (stomach hurts). Compare: 아프다 (to be sick/hurt — general) vs 쑤시다 (to throb/ache — more specific physical sensation).
**-아/어서** (cause): 아파**서** 피곤하다 (because it hurts, I'm tired). **-아/어서** links two clauses where the first is the reason for the second. It cannot be used with the same clause pattern as **-기 때문에** when the main clause has an imperative or suggestive ending — **-아서** is more casual and integrated. Compare: **-기 때문에** (because — more formal, can be used anywhere).

sentences.base_language: Lately he had been working a lot and wasn't sleeping well.
sentences.target_language: 요즘 일을 너무 많이 하고 있었고, 잠도 잘 못 자고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Yojeum il-eul neomu mani hago isseotgo, jam-do jal mot jago isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 있었다** = was doing (past progressive): 하**고 있었다** (was doing), 자**고 있었다** (was sleeping). Korean past progressive combines the connector **-고** + **있다** (to be/exist) in the past tense **있었다**. This differs from the simple past: 했다 (did — completed action) vs 하**고 있었다** (was doing — ongoing).
**못** = cannot, unable to: **못** 자다 (cannot sleep). **못** precedes the verb and negates ability or possibility. Compare: **안** 자다 (does not sleep — choice or habit) vs **못** 자다 (cannot sleep — inability). **잠도 잘 못 자다** literally 'sleep too cannot sleep well' — the colloquial stacking of 잘 (well) and 못 (cannot) is very natural.

sentences.base_language: He decided to go to the hospital near his home.
sentences.target_language: 그는 집 근처에 있는 병원에 가기로 결정했다.
sentences.romanized: Geu-neun jip geuncheo-e inneun byeongwon-e gagi-ro gyeoljeonghae-otda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기로 결정하다/하다** = to decide to do: 가**기로** 결정하다 (decide to go). **-기로** nominalizes the verb and marks it as the decision/plan: 먹**기로** 했다 (decided to eat), 공부**하기로** 했다 (decided to study). Compare: **-기로 하다** (to decide/arrange — slightly more casual) vs **-기로 결정하다** (to decide — more deliberate). **-기로 했다** is one of the most natural ways to express a resolution.
**집 근처에 있는 병원** — noun modifier with **-(으)ㄴ/는**: 있**는** 병원 (a hospital that exists/is located). Present tense action verbs use **-는** as a modifier: 달리**는** 사람 (a running person). Descriptive verbs (adjectives) use **-(으)ㄴ**: 작**은** 병원 (a small hospital). **있다** uses **-는**: 근처에 있**는** 병원 (a hospital that is nearby).

sentences.base_language: He washed his face and got dressed, but felt too weak to eat breakfast.
sentences.target_language: 세수를 하고 옷을 입었지만, 아침을 먹기에는 너무 기운이 없었다.
sentences.romanized: Sesu-reul hago os-eul ibeotjiman, achim-eul meokgi-e-neun neomu gi-un-i eopseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기에는** = for doing, in terms of doing: 먹**기에는** 너무 기운이 없다 (there is too little energy for eating). **-기에** (because of doing / for doing) + **는** (contrast/topic) creates a frame for evaluation or comparison: 이야기하**기에는** 시간이 부족하다 (time is insufficient for talking), 걷**기에는** 너무 멀다 (too far for walking).
**기운이 없다** = to have no energy, to feel weak: **기운** (energy/vitality) + **이 없다** (to not exist). A very natural colloquial expression: 기운**이** 없어서 아무것도 못 했다 (had no energy so couldn't do anything). Compare: **피곤하다** (to be tired — more about fatigue) vs **기운이 없다** (to have no energy — more about vitality and strength).

sentences.base_language: The hospital was two subway stops from his apartment.
sentences.target_language: 병원은 아파트에서 지하철로 두 정거장 거리에 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Byeongwon-eun apateu-eseo jihacheol-ro du jeonggeojang geori-e isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**에서** (from) vs **에** (at/to): **아파트에서** = from the apartment. **에서** marks the starting point of a distance or departure: 서울**에서** 부산까지 (from Seoul to Busan). Compare: **에** marks a destination or static location: 병원**에** 있다 (is at the hospital).
**-(으)로** for means of transport: 지하철**로** (by subway). **-(으)로** expresses the means or method: 버스**로** 가다 (go by bus), 걸어서 가다 (go on foot — note: walking uses **-아/어서** form of 걷다, not 로). **지하철로 두 정거장 거리** = two stops' distance by subway — a very natural way to describe proximity in Korean urban settings.

sentences.base_language: At the reception, a nurse asked him: "How can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: 접수처에서, 간호사가 「무엇을 도와드릴까요?」라고 물었다.
sentences.romanized: Jeopsucheo-eseo, ganhosa-ga 'Mueot-eul dowadeurilkkayo?' rago mureotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**도와드리다** = to help (humble/respectful form): **드리다** is the humble form of **주다** (to give). It is used when the action benefits someone of higher status: 도와**드리다** (help — to you/them, humble), 알려**드리다** (inform — humble), 가져다**드리다** (bring — humble). Compare: 도와**주다** (help — neutral), 도와**드리다** (help — humble, shows respect to recipient).
**-(으)ㄹ까요?** = shall I ~?, would you like me to ~?: 도와드릴**까요?** (shall I help you?). **-(으)ㄹ까요** expresses an offer or tentative question about the future. In the first person it's an offer: 제가 할**까요?** (shall I do it?). In the second person it asks about the listener's preference: 같이 갈**까요?** (shall we go together?).

sentences.base_language: He replied: "My head and stomach hurt, and I feel completely exhausted."
sentences.target_language: 「머리와 배가 아프고, 완전히 지쳐 있어요」라고 대답했다.
sentences.romanized: 'Meori wa bae ga apeuge, wanjeonhi jichyeo isseoyo' rago daedaphaeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**지쳐 있다** = to be exhausted (resultant state): **지치다** (to become exhausted) → 지쳐 **있다** (to be in a state of exhaustion). The **-아/어 있다** construction expresses a resultant state: 앉아 **있다** (is sitting), 열려 **있다** (is open), 지쳐 **있다** (is exhausted). This differs from **-고 있다** (is doing — active progressive): 지치**고 있다** would mean 'is in the process of becoming exhausted' — unusual in speech.
**-고** (connector): 아프**고** (hurts and ~). **-고** connects two clauses or verb phrases with a neutral 'and': 먹**고** 마시다 (eat and drink), 아프**고** 피곤하다 (sick and tired). It does not imply a causal relationship (compare: **-아서** which implies cause). The order matters: the first clause generally precedes the second in time or logic.

sentences.base_language: A doctor examined him and asked: "Do you exercise regularly? How is your diet?"
sentences.target_language: 의사가 그를 진찰하고 「운동을 규칙적으로 하세요? 식사는 어떻게 하고 있어요?」라고 물었다.
sentences.romanized: Uisa-ga geu-reul jinchalago 'Undong-eul gyuchikjeogeuro haseyo? Siksa-neun eotteoke hago isseoyo?' rago mureotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**규칙적으로** — adverb from noun + **-적(이다)** + **-으로**: 규칙 (rule) + **적** (adjectivizing) + **으로** (as/by means of). The **-적으로** suffix converts nouns into adverbs: 정기**적으로** (regularly), 적극**적으로** (actively), 구체**적으로** (concretely). This pattern is very productive in formal and academic Korean, mirroring the Japanese **〜的に** pattern.
**식사는 어떻게 하고 있어요?** = how are you managing your meals?: **식사** (meals) + **는** (topic/contrast) + **어떻게** (how) + **하고 있다** (be doing). **어떻게 하다** is a versatile phrase for asking about methods or habits: 보통 어떻게 **하세요?** (how do you usually do it?). Note the **는** after 식사 — it subtly contrasts diet with exercise (and as for your diet…).

sentences.base_language: He admitted he had been eating a lot of junk food and barely resting.
sentences.target_language: 정크푸드를 너무 많이 먹어 왔고, 거의 쉬지 못했다고 인정했다.
sentences.romanized: Jeongkeu-pudeu-reul neomu mani meogeo watgo, geoui swiji mothaetdago injeonghaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 오다** = to have been doing up to now: 먹어 **왔다** (had been eating — up to the present reference point). **-아/어 오다** expresses an action or state that has continued from some point in the past up to now. Compare: **-아/어 가다** (to continue doing into the future): 점점 나아**가고 있다** (is gradually getting better — going forward). **오다** (come) and **가다** (go) attach directionality to the aspect.
**거의 -지 못하다** = to barely be able to do: 거의 쉬**지 못했다** (barely managed to rest). **거의** (almost, nearly) + **-지 못하다** (cannot do) = 'could barely do.' Compare: **거의** 다 했다 (almost finished — with positive verb) vs **거의** 못 했다 (barely managed — with negation). **-다고 인정하다** = to admit that ~: **-다고** is the quotation form of the declarative -다 ending.

sentences.base_language: The doctor said: "You need more rest and a better diet. Drink plenty of water."
sentences.target_language: 「더 잘 쉬어야 하고, 식사도 개선해야 해요. 물도 많이 드세요.」라고 의사가 말했다.
sentences.romanized: 'Deo jal swieo-ya hago, siksa-do gaeseonhae-ya haeyo. Mul-do mani deuseyo.' rago uisa-ga malhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어야 하다** = must do, have to do: 쉬어**야 하다** (must rest), 개선해**야 하다** (must improve). This is one of the core obligation patterns in Korean. Compare: **-아/어야 되다** (must — slightly more colloquial; **되다** = to become/work): 먹어**야 돼요** (I have to eat). Both are common; **-아야 하다** sounds slightly more formal.
**드세요** — honorific imperative of **드시다** (honorific form of 먹다/마시다): 물을 **드세요** (please drink water — respectful). **드시다** is the honorific verb for eating and drinking, used when showing respect to the listener. Compare: 먹어요 (eat — neutral polite), 드세요 (eat/drink — respectful). Using **드시다** with patients, elders, or customers is standard.

sentences.base_language: He felt a little embarrassed, but also relieved to know it wasn't serious.
sentences.target_language: 조금 부끄러웠지만, 심각하지 않다는 것을 알고 안심이 됐다.
sentences.romanized: Jogeum bukkeureoweotjiman, simgak-haji anhdaneun geot-eul algo ansim-i dwaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**안심이 되다** = to feel relieved, to be reassured: **안심** (peace of mind) + **이 되다** (to become). The **이/가 되다** construction expresses a state that comes about: 안심**이 됐다** (felt relieved), 걱정이 됐다 (became worried), 행복이 됐다 (became happy). Compare: **안심하다** (to feel at ease — with verb 하다) vs **안심이 되다** (to become reassured — spontaneous change).
**-다는 것을 알다** = to know that ~: 심각하지 않**다는 것을** 알다 (to know that it is not serious). **-다는 것** nominalizes a reported fact: 그가 왔**다는 것을** 알았다 (knew that he had come). The **-다는** combines the quotative **-다고** with the attributive **-는** to create a noun-modifying reported clause — a very important grammar pattern for complex sentences.

sentences.base_language: He left the hospital and found a grocery store nearby.
sentences.target_language: 병원을 나와서 근처에 슈퍼마켓이 있는 것을 발견했다.
sentences.romanized: Byeongwon-eul nawaseo geuncheo-e syupeomaket-i inneun geot-eul balgyeonhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**병원을 나오다** = to come out of / leave the hospital: **나오다** (to come out) takes the place you exit with **을/를**: 방**을** 나오다 (come out of the room), 집**을** 나오다 (leave the house). Compare: **나가다** (to go out — moving away from the speaker's perspective): 밖으로 나**가다** vs **나오다** (come out — toward or at the speaker's location). The distinction mirrors 가다/오다.
**있는 것을 발견하다** = to discover that something exists: **있는** (existing, modifier) + **것** (thing, nominalizer) + **을** (object) + **발견하다** (discover). **발견하다** = to discover/find (implies something previously unknown). Compare: **찾다** (to find — what you were looking for) vs **발견하다** (to discover — unexpected or newly noticed).

sentences.base_language: He bought vegetables, fish, and fruit — no snacks this time.
sentences.target_language: 채소, 생선, 과일을 샀다. 이번에는 과자는 사지 않았다.
sentences.romanized: Chaeso, saengseon, gwa-il-eul satda. Ibeon-e-neun gwaja-neun saji anhatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**이번** (this time/this occasion) vs **지금** (now): **이번** (이 this + 번 time/occasion) refers to this particular instance: **이번** 시험 (this exam), **이번에는** (this time). Compare: **지금** (right now — time on the clock or in the moment): 지금 뭐 해요? (what are you doing right now?). **이번에는** + negative is a natural way to express 'not this time': 이번에는 참았다 (held back this time).
**과자는 사지 않았다** — the **는** on **과자** creates contrast: as for snacks (unlike the other things) — did not buy. The **-지 않다** negation pattern: verb stem + **-지** + **않다**: 사**지** 않다 (don't/didn't buy). Compare with the shorter negation **안** + verb: **안** 샀다. **-지 않다** is slightly more formal and emphatic; **안** + verb is more colloquial.

sentences.base_language: Back home, he made a simple soup with vegetables and chicken.
sentences.target_language: 집에 돌아와서 채소와 닭고기로 간단한 국을 끓였다.
sentences.romanized: Jib-e dorawaseo chaeso wa dak-gogi-ro gandanhan guk-eul kkeulhyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**돌아오다** = to return, to come back: 집에 **돌아오다** (come back home). **돌아** (turning) + **오다** (come) — the 돌아- prefix implies a return to origin. Compare: **돌아가다** (go back — away from speaker's perspective): 고향에 **돌아가다** (go back to one's hometown). The speaker perspective distinction (오다/가다) also applies to compound verbs: 돌아**오다** vs 돌아**가다**.
**-(으)로** for means or material: 닭고기**로** 국을 끓이다 (make soup with chicken). **-(으)로** marks the material used: 쌀**로** 밥을 짓다 (make rice from rice), 나무**로** 집을 짓다 (build a house with wood). Also marks direction and means of transport. Here it functions as 'using/with' — distinguishing the ingredient from the direct object (the soup).

sentences.base_language: While cooking, he called his friend and told her about the doctor's visit.
sentences.target_language: 요리를 하면서 친구에게 전화해서 병원에 다녀온 이야기를 했다.
sentences.romanized: Yori-reul haminseо chingu-ege jeonhwahаeseo byeongwon-e danyeo-on iyagi-reul haetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서** = while doing (simultaneous actions, same subject): 요리를 하**면서** (while cooking). **-면서** implies the two actions happen at the same time and the subject is the same. Compare: **-는 동안** (while / during the time that ~ — emphasises the duration, and subjects can differ): 내가 자는 **동안** 그가 왔다 (while I was sleeping, he came).
**다녀오다** = to go somewhere and come back: 병원에 **다녀오다** (to have gone to the hospital and returned). This compound verb (**다니다** go back and forth + **오다** come) is used for trips to places and back: 시장에 **다녀왔어요** (I've been to the market and back). A very natural and common Korean verb for completed outings — much more natural than saying 병원에 가고 왔다.

sentences.base_language: His friend laughed and said: "I always told you to take better care of yourself!"
sentences.target_language: 친구가 웃으면서 「항상 몸 관리 잘 하라고 했잖아!」라고 말했다.
sentences.romanized: Chingu-ga useuminseо 'Hangsang mom gwanri jal harago haetjana!' rago malhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-라고 하다** = to say to do (indirect command): 잘 하**라고** 했다 (told [someone] to do well). **-라고** is the indirect quotation of an imperative: 가**라고** 했다 (said to go), 먹**으라고** 했다 (told to eat). Compare: **-다고 하다** (says that — declarative) vs **-(으)라고 하다** (says to do — imperative) vs **-(으)냐고 하다** (asks whether — interrogative).
**-잖아** = you know / didn't I (tag): 했**잖아** (I told you, didn't I? / I said so, you know). **-잖아(요)** is a very common ending that reminds the listener of something they should already know, often with a tone of 'I told you so': 비가 온다**잖아요** (it's raining, you know), 맞**잖아요** (it's right, isn't it?). It's more assertive than **-지요** (right?) and implies shared knowledge.

sentences.base_language: After eating, he felt much better — his energy was returning.
sentences.target_language: 식사 후에 기분이 훨씬 나아졌다. 기운이 다시 돌아오는 느낌이었다.
sentences.romanized: Siksa hu-e gibun-i hwolssin naajyeotda. Gi-un-i dasi doraoneun neukkimi-eotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어지다** = to become, to get (change of state): 나아**지다** (to get better — from 낫다, irregular). **-아/어지다** attaches to descriptive verb stems to express a gradual change: 좋아**지다** (to get better/improve), 커**지다** (to get bigger), 힘들어**지다** (to get harder). This is the standard way to express becoming in Korean — do not use 되다 with descriptive verbs in this way.
**돌아오는 느낌** = the feeling of coming back/returning: **돌아오는** (returning — present modifier of action verb) modifies **느낌** (feeling). The **느낌이다** construction (to have the feeling of ~) is very natural in Korean: 꿈 같은 **느낌이다** (feels like a dream), 뭔가 달라진 **느낌이다** (feels like something has changed). Using **느낌** personalizes and softens the statement.

sentences.base_language: He lay down and rested for a couple of hours.
sentences.target_language: 누워서 두어 시간 정도 쉬었다.
sentences.romanized: Nuwoseo dueo sigan jeongdo swieotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**눕다** (to lie down) — irregular **ㅂ** verb: 눕 + 아/어 → 누**워** (irregular — ㅂ changes to 우 before vowel). Other **ㅂ** irregulars: 덥다 → 더**워** (hot), 굽다 → 구**워** (grill), 쉽다 → 쉬**워** (easy). Exception: 잡다 → 잡**아** (grab — regular). The ㅂ irregulars are a common source of errors — memorize them as a group.
**두어** = a couple of, approximately two: **두어 시간** (a couple of hours), **두어 명** (a couple of people). **두어** is a slightly old-fashioned but still natural approximation of 둘 (two). Compare: **몇 시간** (a few hours — indefinite), **두 시간쯤** (about two hours — more precise), **두어 시간** (a couple of hours — natural colloquial approximation). **정도** (approximately, about) stacks with **두어** for additional softening.

sentences.base_language: By evening, his mood had completely improved and he felt calm and content.
sentences.target_language: 저녁이 될 무렵에는 기분이 완전히 나아져 있었고, 편안하고 만족스러운 느낌이었다.
sentences.romanized: Jeonyeok-i doel mureop-e-neun gibun-i wanjeonhi naajyeo isseotgo, pyeonanago manjokseureoun neukkimi-eotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 무렵** = around the time when ~: 저녁이 될 **무렵** (around the time it was becoming evening). **무렵** (around/about — for time) always follows a modifier clause: 해가 질 **무렵** (around sunset), 도착할 **무렵** (around the time of arrival). Compare: **-을 때** (when — more precise, at the time): 도착**했을 때** (when I arrived — exact point) vs 도착할 **무렵** (around the time of arrival — approximate).
**나아져 있다** = to be in a state of having gotten better: **나아지다** + **-아/어 있다** (resultant state). **-아/어 있다** expresses a state resulting from a change: 앉아 **있다** (is sitting), 닫혀 **있다** (is closed), 나아져 **있다** (is in a better state). The past form **나아져 있었다** = had gotten better (and was still better at that time) — essential for describing states at a past reference point.

sentences.base_language: He promised himself to take better care of his body from now on.
sentences.target_language: 그는 앞으로 몸을 더 잘 챙기겠다고 스스로에게 다짐했다.
sentences.romanized: Geu-neun apeu-ro mom-eul deo jal chaenggi-getdago seuseuro-ege dajimhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**챙기다** = to take care of, to look after, to make sure to get/do: 몸을 **챙기다** (take care of one's body/health), 밥은 챙겨 먹어야 해 (you need to make sure you eat). **챙기다** implies attentive, deliberate care — it has a nuance of 'not neglecting.' Very common and natural in Korean for health and self-care contexts. Compare: **돌보다** (to look after — often for others: children, patients) vs **챙기다** (to take care of — often for oneself or things you need to remember).
**다짐하다** = to make a resolution, to pledge to oneself: 스스로에게 **다짐하다** (make a resolution to oneself). **다짐** implies a firm inner commitment — stronger than 결심 (決心) in its personal, self-directed nuance. Compare: **결심하다** (to resolve/decide — can be about external goals too) vs **다짐하다** (to pledge to oneself — more personal and internal). **앞으로** (going forward/from now on) is the natural Korean equivalent of 'from now on' in resolving expressions.
